14: A fulfillment: 'Sing aloud, O daughter of Zion; shout, O Israel! Rejoice and exult with all your heart, O daughter of Jerusalem! The LORD has taken away the judgments against you ... The King of Israel, the LORD, is in your midst; you shall never again fear evil.' This call to rejoice because YHWH is in the midst of his people reaches its fulfillment in the incarnation. Luke's Annunciation (Zephaniah 1:28-33) and the angels' song at the Nativity (Zephaniah 2:10-14: I bring you good news of great joy) are the NT's recasting of Zephaniah's joy-cry: rejoice, for the Lord is with you. The movement from Zephaniah to Luke is the movement from prophetic announcement to historical fulfillment: the divine King who was to come 'in your midst' comes as the infant in Bethlehem, and then the risen Lord who sends the Spirit so that YHWH is 'in the midst' of the church (Matthew 18:20; John 14:23).
1: A shadow: ‘Woe to the rebellious, defiled, oppressing city!’ The triple woe against Jerusalem — rebellious, defiled, oppressive — is the same accusation Jesus makes in Matthew 23’s seven woes. Christ enters the woe-city and becomes the recipient of the woe (Galatians 3:13: he became a curse for us) so that its inhabitants might be freed from it. He was neither rebellious, nor defiled, nor oppressive: “he committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth” (1 Peter 2:22).
2: A shadow: ‘She has not listened to any voice; she has accepted no correction. She has not trusted in the LORD; she has not drawn near to her God.’ The four-part indictment — deaf, unteachable, faithless, distant — is the OT profile of covenant failure applied to Christ’s generation: “He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him” (John 1:11). In Christ all four failures are reversed: he listened perfectly (John 5:30), accepted the Father’s will including the cross (Luke 22:42), trusted absolutely (Hebrews 2:13), and was in constant communion with the Father (John 10:30).
3: A shadow: ‘Her officials within her are roaring lions; her judges are wolves of the evening that leave no bone until morning.’ The predatory leaders — lion-officials and wolf-judges — are the anti-shepherds against whom Christ sets himself as the Good Shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep (John 10:11): the anti-predator who is himself consumed so the flock is spared. The leaders who devour in Zephaniah are judged by the shepherd who is devoured for the sheep.
4: A shadow: ‘Her prophets are fickle, treacherous men; her priests profane what is holy; they do violence to the law.’ The corrupt prophets and priests answered by Christ who fulfills all three offices: the true prophet (Deuteronomy 18:15; Acts 3:22) who speaks only what the Father gives him (John 12:49); the true priest (Hebrews 5:5-6) who does not profane but fulfills the holy; the true interpreter of the law (Matthew 5:17: I have not come to abolish the law but to fulfill it).
5: A revelation of God: ‘The LORD within her is righteous; he does no wrong. Morning after morning he brings his justice to light; he never fails.’ YHWH’s morning-by-morning faithfulness contrasted with Jerusalem’s shameless injustice is the OT theodicy: God is righteous; his people are not. The NT resolution: “God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God” (2 Corinthians 5:21). The righteous God of Zephaniah 3:5 does not compromise his righteousness — he transfers it to his people through Christ.
6: A shadow: ‘I have cut off nations; their battlements are in ruins; I have laid waste their streets.’ YHWH rehearsing his judgments against the nations as a warning to Jerusalem is the eschatological pedagogy Paul extends: “Now these things happened to them as an example, but they were written down for our instruction” (1 Corinthians 10:11). The desolated cities of history are sermons in stone; the cross is the ultimate lesson written in flesh — what the Day of the LORD does, borne by Christ so his people learn without perishing.
7: A shadow: ‘I said, “Surely you will fear me; you will accept correction.” But all the more they were eager to make all their deeds corrupt.’ The divine expectation of repentance repeatedly disappointed is the OT pattern Jesus echoes in the parable of the tenants: the owner sends servants expecting fruit; each is rejected; finally he sends his son (Matthew 21:33-39). The divine patience that kept expecting response reached its ultimate expression in the Son — and the rejection of the Son became the means of redemption (Acts 4:27-28).
8: A direct anticipation: ‘“Therefore wait for me, declares the LORD, for the day when I rise up to seize the prey.”’ “Wait for me” (chakwu li) — YHWH calling his people to patient expectation — is the OT command of eschatological patience. Paul translates this: “we ourselves groan inwardly as we wait eagerly for adoption as sons, the redemption of our bodies” (Romans 8:23). The rising of YHWH to act points to the resurrection — Christ rising to seize what death held and gather the nations (Matthew 28:18-20). The “wait for me” of Zephaniah is the “come, Lord Jesus” of Revelation 22:20.
9: A fulfillment: ‘At that time I will give the peoples a pure language, so that all of them may call on the name of the LORD and serve him with one purpose.’ The pure language given to all peoples is the reversal of Babel (Genesis 11:7). Pentecost is the partial fulfillment: all nations hear the gospel in their own language (Acts 2:8-11) and call on the name of the Lord (Acts 2:21). The final fulfillment: “a great multitude from every nation, from all tribes and peoples and languages, standing before the throne and before the Lamb” (Revelation 7:9) — united worship, one name, one purpose.
10: A shadow: ‘From beyond the rivers of Cush my worshipers, the daughter of my dispersed ones, shall bring my offering.’ Worshippers from the farthest reaches of the world bringing offerings to YHWH — the OT eschatological vision of universal worship beginning in Acts (the Ethiopian eunuch, Acts 8) and consummating in Revelation (worshipers from every nation, Revelation 5:9-10). Christ’s commission extends exactly to these “ends of the earth” (Acts 1:8).
11: A shadow: ‘On that day you shall not be put to shame because of the deeds by which you have rebelled against me; for then I will remove from your midst your proudly exultant ones.’ The shame removed — no longer condemned for rebellion — is the NT’s “there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus” (Romans 8:1). The proud removed from the holy mountain anticipates the purification of the new Jerusalem: “nothing unclean will ever enter it” (Revelation 21:27). Christ’s work removes both the guilt and the residual pride of his people (Philippians 1:6).
12: A direct anticipation: ‘I will leave in your midst a people who are humble and poor; they will find refuge in the name of the LORD.’ The remnant who survive the Day are specifically the humble and poor (ani and dal). This is the OT template for the Beatitudes: “Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven” (Matthew 5:3); “Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth” (Matthew 5:5). The “name of the LORD” in which they find refuge is the name of Jesus: “there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved” (Acts 4:12).
13: A shadow: ‘They shall do no injustice and speak no lies, nor shall there be found in their mouth a deceitful tongue. For they shall graze and lie down, and none shall make them afraid.’ The remnant without injustice or deceit, lying down in peace — the OT profile of the redeemed community. Revelation applies this to the 144,000 (Revelation 14:5: “in their mouth no lie was found, for they are blameless”). Christ is the pattern: “no deceit was found in his mouth” (1 Peter 2:22), and his people are being conformed to the same truthfulness (Ephesians 4:15: speaking the truth in love).
15: A direct anticipation: ‘The LORD has taken away the judgments against you; he has cleared away your enemies. The King of Israel, the LORD, is in your midst; you shall never again fear evil.’ Three declarations: judgment removed, enemies cleared, the King of Israel — YHWH himself — present in the midst. The NT fulfillment is the incarnation: (1) “there is now no condemnation” (Romans 8:1); (2) “he disarmed the rulers and authorities” (Colossians 2:15); (3) “the Word became flesh and dwelt among us” (John 1:14). The King of Israel present in the midst is specifically Jesus — YHWH incarnate, whose presence makes fear impossible (1 John 4:18).
16: A shadow: ‘On that day it shall be said to Jerusalem: “Do not fear, O Zion; do not let your hands go limp.”’ The eschatological “do not fear” addressed to restored Zion is the most frequent command in the Bible, spoken supremely by Jesus: “Do not be afraid, little flock, for it is your Father’s good pleasure to give you the kingdom” (Luke 12:32); “Fear not, I am the first and the last and the living one” (Revelation 1:17-18). Hebrews 12:12 uses the same idiom: “lift your drooping hands and strengthen your weak knees.” Christ’s resurrection is the ground for this strengthening.
17: A revelation of God: ‘The LORD your God is in your midst — a warrior who saves. He will rejoice over you with joy; he will be still in his love; he will exult over you with singing.’ Zephaniah 3:17 is the OT’s most intimate portrait of divine joy: YHWH not merely tolerating his people but singing over them. The NT’s three forms of this joy: (1) Jesus’s own joy in his disciples — “that my joy may be in you, and that your joy may be full” (John 15:11); (2) the Father’s joy over the returning prodigal (Luke 15:20); (3) the eschatological joy at the wedding supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:7). The warrior who saves and rejoices is Christ — the divine warrior whose warfare is love and whose victory song is the resurrection.
18: A shadow: ‘I will gather those of you who grieve over the appointed festival, so that you will no longer suffer reproach.’ The gathering of those who grieve over missed sacred festivals — the longing to worship that exile has blocked — is answered by Christ’s invitation: “Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest” (Matthew 11:28). He is himself the festival (1 Corinthians 5:7-8: Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed) — all the appointed feasts are fulfilled in him, and all mourning over missed worship is answered in his presence.
19: A shadow: ‘Behold, at that time I will deal with all your oppressors. And I will save the lame and gather the outcast, and I will change their shame into praise and renown in all the earth.’ The divine advocacy for the lame and outcast — shame turned to praise — is the eschatological vision Jesus enacts in his healing ministry: he heals the lame (Matthew 11:5), gathers the outcast (Luke 15), and publicly honors the shamed (the hemorrhaging woman honored as “daughter,” Mark 5:34). Every healing is a firstfruit of the final gathering when all shame becomes praise.
20: A fulfillment: ‘At that time I will bring you home, when I gather you together; I will give you a name and praise among all the peoples of the earth, when I restore your fortunes before your eyes, says the LORD.’ Zephaniah closes with homecoming. The NT’s form: “In my Father’s house are many rooms ... I go to prepare a place for you. And ... I will come again and will take you to myself” (John 14:2-3). The “home” Zephaniah promises is the Father’s house Jesus prepares; the “name given to all peoples” is the name above every name (Philippians 2:9-11); the restored fortunes are the inheritance “imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, kept in heaven” (1 Peter 1:4).
